title: Air Conditioning in Berlin - Quick Summary
items: **You cannot assume a Berlin room has AC.** Big chains and newer hotels (Motel One, Adina, larger Steigenberger) usually do. Small family hotels, hostels and most old-building apartment rentals often have **no air conditioning at all**.
**Why:** fewer than one in twelve German homes has active AC. Berlin was built for cold winters, so cooling was never standard, and old protected buildings are hard to retrofit.

**The German method beats AC most days:** shutters (_Rollladen_) or curtains down and windows shut through the hot afternoon, then windows wide open for cross-ventilation (_Stoßlüften_) once the evening cools to ~13-18°C.

**Get a fan on day one.** They sell out in a heatwave; many hotels lend one from reception if you ask. Point it across an open window at night. A lower-floor or courtyard-facing room stays cooler than a top-floor attic.

**Escape the midday heat:** Museum Island and the Humboldt Forum hold their cool, and stations/malls like Hauptbahnhof are free and cold. **The U-Bahn is NOT air conditioned** (BVG), so platforms are among the hottest spots. Otherwise, do as locals do and go to a lake or pool.

**Booking in July/August?** Filter for air conditioning and confirm by email if it matters, or pick a low-floor, courtyard room. Berlin heat comes in short, sharp episodes; check heat warnings at warnung.bund.de.
